steps.title: Identify the hypothesis's prediction
steps.content: The second team predicts that in low-visibility conditions with large vortices, intermediate-headed fish will be more likely than narrow-headed fish to detect obstructions. More likely to detect = fewer collisions. A supporting finding must compare both types in exactly those conditions and show intermediate-headed fish performing better.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — A study using obstructions that created large vortices in low-visibility conditions found that the intermediate-headed Lobocheilos rhabdoura bumped into the obstructions just as often as the narrow-headed lookdown (Selene vomer) did.: equal performance — this contradicts the hypothesis rather than supporting it. ✗
  • Choice B — A study using obstructions that created large vortices in low-visibility conditions found that the lookdown (Selene vomer), which has a relatively narrow head, bumped into more than half of the obstructions.: only tests narrow-headed fish; we learn nothing about how intermediate-headed fish compare, so the hypothesis is unaddressed. ✗
  • Choice C — A study using obstructions that created large vortices in low-visibility conditions found that the narrow-headed lookdown (Selene vomer) bumped into obstructions more often than the intermediate-headed Lobocheilos rhabdoura did.: directly compares both fish types in the specified conditions and shows intermediate-headed fish detecting better (fewer collisions) — exactly what the hypothesis predicts. ✓
  • Choice D — A study using obstructions that created large vortices in low-visibility conditions found that some specimens of the intermediate-headed Lobocheilos rhabdoura bumped into the obstructions more often than other specimens of the same fish did.: measures within-species variation only; no comparison to narrow-headed fish, so the hypothesis is untested. ✗
That is the correct answer.
